name: Wu Song
description: Wu Song, a prominent figure in Chinese folklore and one of the 108 Stars of Destiny in the classic novel *Water Margin*, is revered as a heroic outlaw and a symbol of courage, justice, and righteous rebellion against oppressive forces. He is often depicted as a skilled martial artist, possessing immense strength and unwavering determination. Wu Song's legendary exploits include his famous slaying of a tiger with his bare hands on Jingyang Ridge, an act that cemented his reputation as a formidable warrior. His story is intertwined with themes of loyalty, brotherhood, and the fight against corruption within the Song Dynasty. Wu Song embodies the spirit of the common people rising against injustice and tyranny, making him a powerful and enduring symbol in Chinese culture. He is worshipped as a deity in some traditions, particularly among those who seek protection and courage in the face of adversity. His narrative serves as a potent reminder of the importance of integrity and standing up for what is right, even in the face of overwhelming odds.
subtitle: Tiger Slayer

keyMyths.title: Wu Song Fights the Tiger
keyMyths.description: While traveling through Jingyang Ridge, Wu Song ignored warnings about a man-eating tiger. After drinking heavily at a tavern, he encountered the ferocious beast. Despite his drunken state, Wu Song bravely fought the tiger with his bare hands, eventually subduing and killing it. This act made him a local hero and earned him a position as a constable.

keyMyths.title: Wu Song Kills Pan Jinlian and Ximen Qing
keyMyths.description: After discovering that his brother, Wu Dalang, was poisoned by his adulterous wife, Pan Jinlian, and her lover, Ximen Qing, Wu Song sought revenge. He meticulously gathered evidence and, in a dramatic confrontation, killed both Pan Jinlian and Ximen Qing. While his actions were considered murder, many viewed them as justified retribution for their crimes.

keyMyths.title: Wu Song Becomes a Monk
keyMyths.description: Following his act of vengeance, Wu Song was exiled. During his exile, he sought refuge in a monastery and became a monk. However, his rebellious nature and inability to adhere to monastic rules led him to eventually leave the monastery and join the Liangshan outlaws. This transition marks his complete embrace of the outlaw life.

sources.author: Shi Nai'an
sources.description: The primary source for the legends and stories of Wu Song and the other Liangshan heroes.
sources.text: The primary source for the legends and stories of Wu Song and the other Liangshan heroes.

sources.author: Buck, Pearl S.
sources.description: An English translation and adaptation of *Water Margin*, providing a Western audience with access to the Wu Song narrative.

sources.author: Richard G. Irwin
sources.work: The Evolution of a Chinese Novel: Shui Hu Chuan
sources.description: An academic analysis of the *Water Margin*, exploring the historical and cultural context of the novel and its characters, including Wu Song.
